channel.title: Kubernetes on jmpargana
channel.description: Recent content in Kubernetes on jmpargana

item.title: CKAD - 2 - Kubernetes API Resources
item.description:
While Kubernetes is far more than just a server, thinking of it as a REST API server is incredibly helpful for understanding how to interact with cluster resources.

In this post, I’ll explore how we can interact with Kubernetes as a server and how to discover its API.

Proxy

To make it easier for you to see how Kubernetes functions as a server, run:

> kubectl proxy

Now we can call the API to navigate through its endpoints. Calling the root endpoint shows all available paths:

item.title: MiniTube - PKI & rootCA + LB Setup
item.description:
This post is part of my MiniTube series. Check out other related posts if you find this content useful.

Problem

Let’s start with the problem. The Web Crypto API is only available in secure contexts (HTTPS) or localhost.

While developing the frontend for my MiniTube application, the function I was using to generate a random UUID (crypto.randomUUID()) stopped working once I deployed the container in Kubernetes.

Solution

The solution wasn’t really a solution, but rather an opportunity to understand how to set up TLS locally.

item.title: CKAD - 1 - Foundations
item.description:
Welcome! In this series, you’ll read along my journey to CKAD certification. Expect high-level knowledge sharing and hopefully some useful tips.

Picking the course

Visiting the Linux Foundation site, you’ll see a suggested package that includes a course, annual subscription, and more.

After getting advice from colleagues who are already certified, I decided to follow a different course instead on Udemy.

Not only does this have close to 5-star reviews, it’s significantly cheaper. I purchased it at an 80% discount (around $11).
